Species.reason: The paper studies soybean and explicitly names the species as soybean.

Species_Latin_Name.reason: The title/abstract explicitly states soybean [Glycine max (L.) Merr.].

Gene_Origin_Species.reason: GmIF6H1 is identified and characterized from soybean/Glycine max.

Variety.reason: The paper states most soybean lines were in Williams 82 (W82) background, and hairy root assays used Kefeng-1 (KF-1/Kefeng-1).

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The manuscript gives the locus as Glyma.11G108300 / Glyma.11g108300 depending on formatting. The exact lowercase accession string in the JSON is close but not consistently identical to the paper's capitalization.

Omics_Data.reason: Results and Methods support mGWAS using prior resequencing SNP data with 53 soja accessions around 1× coverage; LC-qToF-MS seed isoflavonoid profiling of 727 accessions in 2013 and 2014 multi-location samples; transcriptome profiling in infected hypocotyls; and LC-QQQ-MS/MS isotope tracing with daidzein-d6. 'accession numbers NA' is not stated but merely indicates absence, so the substantive content is supported.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The gene is explicitly placed in soybean isoflavonoid/glycitein biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper shows GmIF6H1 acts on daidzein to produce 6-hydroxydaidzein leading to glycitein/glycitin-type isoflavonoids.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Results/Discussion describe daidzein being partitioned between glycitein-type isoflavonoids via GmIF6H1 and glyceollins via GmI2'H, i.e., a branch point.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The authors explicitly redesignate GmCYP76F17 as GmIF6H1, isoflavone 6-hydroxylase 1.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper identifies it as a cytochrome P450, CYP76F17, in the CYP76 subfamily.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Subcellular localization analysis showed GmCYP76F17-GFP colocalized with an ER marker.

Expression_Pattern.reason: The paper states GmIF6H1 expression mirrors glycitein accumulation, preferentially in mature roots and hypocotyls, and remained unchanged upon P. sojae infection.

Promoters.reason: Methods and Results state overexpression lines were generated using the CaMV 35S promoter; endogenous/native region was analyzed in haplotype analysis.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Biochemical assays and in planta stable isotope tracing demonstrate daidzein is the bona fide substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: Yeast feeding identified the product from daidzein as 6-hydroxydaidzein.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper explicitly states A-ring/C6 hydroxylation of daidzein to 6-hydroxydaidzein, followed by 6-O-methylation to glycitein, and reports in vitro activity toward formononetin and biochanin A.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: 6-Hydroxydaidzein is described as the direct product/intermediate leading to glycitein, i.e., an immediate precursor relationship.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The gene is directly linked to glycitein/glycitein-type isoflavonoids throughout Results.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Glycitein-type compounds are isoflavonoids/isoflavones in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Results show glycitein-type compounds accumulate preferentially in mature roots and hypocotyls; seed glycitein/glycitin/malonylglycitin were also profiled.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results support a defensive role against P. sojae and Discussion/Introduction note soybean isoflavones benefit human health/nutrition.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: All listed treatments appear in Results/Methods: P. sojae infection, daidzein-d6 feeding, germinating hypocotyls, yeast substrate feeding, and metal-ion effects on enzyme activity.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: CRISPR mutants nearly lacked glycitein-type isoflavonoids and had ~1.5-fold increases in daidzin/malonyldaidzin; OE accumulated 6-hydroxydaidzein conjugates and had higher glycitein during infection; both mutant and OE were more susceptible to P. sojae.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper explicitly calls GmIF6H1 a key determinant of glycitein biosynthesis and shows it controls daidzein allocation between glycitein-type compounds and glyceollins.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Most listed methods are supported, but the last clause is wrong: chemical complementation in the paper used exogenous glycitein/glycinein? The Results and P. sojae methods consistently describe complementation with glycinein/glycitein inconsistently due to text issues; the JSON specifically says exogenous glycitein, which is not faithfully supported by the main Results sentence stating glycinein was applied and restored resistance. Because of this mismatch, the full field is not faithfully supported.

Breeding_Application_Value.reason: Abstract/Discussion state the findings offer opportunities for engineering disease-resilient soybean cultivars and precision breeding for isoflavonoid profiles.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly show overexpression lowers glyceollin I during infection and increases susceptibility; both loss and gain of function compromise defense.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Each point is stated in Results/Discussion: former name GmCYP76F17, redesignation to GmIF6H1, T248A haplotype effect, kinetic difference, Km 7.49 ± 0.73 µM, higher in vitro activity toward formononetin/biochanin A but not detected in planta, and trace glycitein in infected mutants suggesting alternative routes.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary accurately condenses the core Results: mGWAS identification, enzyme function/localization, mutant and OE phenotypes, isotope tracing, T248/A248 haplotype effects, and defense tradeoff between glycitein-type metabolites and glyceollins.

corrections.old_value: mGWAS; soybean hairy-root overexpression metabolite profiling; yeast substrate-feeding assay; microsomal enzyme assay and kinetics; ER localization; CRISPR-Cas9 loss-of-function mutants; CaMV35S overexpression lines; in planta stable-isotope tracing with daidzein-d6; pathogen infection assay; chemical complementation with exogenous glycitein
corrections.reason: Most listed methods are supported, but the last clause is wrong: chemical complementation in the paper used exogenous glycitein/glycinein? The Results and P. sojae methods consistently describe complementation with glycinein/glycitein inconsistently due to text issues; the JSON specifically says exogenous glycitein, which is not faithfully supported by the main Results sentence stating glycinein was applied and restored resistance. Because of this mismatch, the full field is not faithfully supported.
